role_summary: First field secretary of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC); pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, Louisiana (1966-2020); president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention; general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc; SCLC organizer from 1960.
ai_reading: Blake's neighbor counts are shallow and broadly distributed (the top match, Clarence Magee at entry 21, appears in only 14 of his 74 passage chunks; Charles McLaurin at entry 17 follows at 13, then Robert G. Clark Jr. at entry 107 and Rosie Head at entry 111). Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132), the SCLC chief of staff Blake names in his own account of their joint Shreveport arrest, surfaces lower in the neighbor list at 10 co-appearances, so the cosine space does not foreground the SCLC-field-and-headquarters pairing that the historical record would predict. The more distinctive reading is on the concept axes. On the Sacred vs Secular axis Blake sits at +0.63 toward Sacred and Theological Framing, the fourth-most-sacred position among all 136 entries, just behind Wyatt Tee Walker at +0.73; for a Baptist pastor whose entire career was church leadership, the embedding reads his testimony as among the most theological in the corpus, and it places the two SCLC clergy together at that sacred pole rather than at the political one. He also sits at +0.78 toward Individual Conscience on the Individual vs Collective axis (fourth-most in the corpus) and toward Nonviolence as Theology at +0.54 on the Nonviolence axis, a placement that aligns with the nonviolence framing of the organization he joined.
biographical_paragraph: While Blake's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is documented in the Louisiana local press at the time of his April 2020 death [src: 1, src: 2]. The Louisiana press obituaries record his birth on May 20, 1934 in Bossier Parish, Louisiana [src: 2]; in his own oral history Blake states he was born in Arkansas and was brought to Louisiana very young, where he was raised on plantations in Madison Parish and north of Shreveport [src: 1]. He attended Bishop College in Marshall, Texas, where Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his commencement address, and joined the Southern Christian Leadership Conference in 1960 as a field secretary, described in the press accounts as the organization's first [src: 1, src: 2]. On October 12, 1960, the year he joined the SCLC staff, a gunman fired into his car as he drove home from his rural church; he counted four bullet holes in the coat he was wearing [src: 1, src: 2]. On September 22, 1963, a week after the Birmingham 16th Street Baptist Church bombing that killed four girls, Blake convened a memorial service at Little Union Baptist Church in Shreveport; in his account, the public-safety commissioner and police officers met him in the church vestibule, beat him about the head, and dragged him onto the lawn, leaving him for dead [src: 1, src: 2]. In 1966 he became pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, the post he would hold for the remainder of his ministry. He served as president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention and as general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc. He died April 8, 2020 in Shreveport at eighty-five [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Blake traces his pastoral method to a saying his father, a sharecropper who could not read or write, taught him on the plantation:
interview_snippets.quote: My daddy taught me that there was a saying that you can lead a horse through the water, but you can't make him drink.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Blake recounts telling his parents he would take a job on Martin Luther King Jr.'s staff that could get him or them killed, and his father's reply:
interview_snippets.quote: I went and said to my dad and mom, the job that I'm going to take may result in my death or injury. But not only that, it may result in your injury or death because I'm your child. I want to apologize for putting you in that position. But you raised me and made me the kind of person I am. And my dad said, I'd be disappointed if you did anything different.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the attempt on his life in the year he joined the SCLC field staff:
interview_snippets.quote: I came on the staff, March 1st, 1960, October 12th. There was an assassination attempt on my life.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Blake describes the moment the shot struck his car on a two-lane highway:
interview_snippets.quote: And I heard this noise. I thought he had thrown a rock, but they had shot in my car.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Blake recounts the September 22, 1963 beating after the memorial service at Little Union Baptist Church:
interview_snippets.quote: And they'd manhandle me and began to beat me inside the vestibule of church, drug me on the outside and every policeman who could get close enough to get a piece of my head with his nice stick. They thought I was dead or appeared to be lifeless and they left me late and on the lawn on the sidewalk. That was September 22nd, 1963.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Blake recalls how the federal Civil Rights Commission characterized the city where he organized:
interview_snippets.quote: However, the Civil Rights Commission said in their report that Shreveport was the most difficult city in the South.

movement_context: The SCLC's Shreveport campaign emerged from the local infrastructure that dentist C.O. Simpkins had built through the mid-1950s, when Simpkins (1925-2019) co-founded SCLC with Martin Luther King Jr. and other ministers and invited King to Shreveport to attend to the city's racial conditions [src: 3, src: 4]. Simpkins's home and dental office were firebombed by the Ku Klux Klan in retaliation; he was driven into temporary exile in New York and would not return to Louisiana politics for another quarter century [src: 4]. The 1960 hiring of Harry Blake as SCLC's first field secretary placed a Shreveport-based organizer inside the new SCLC field apparatus at the moment that the SCLC's October annual convention came to Shreveport itself; the shooting attempt on Blake's life after the convention belongs to the same arc of Klan and police-aligned violence that displaced Simpkins [src: 2]. The September 22, 1963 beating at Little Union Baptist Church is the most-documented single incident from the Shreveport organizing campaign of those years. Public Safety Commissioner George D'Artois told Blake that any participant in the planned march from Booker T. Washington High School to Little Union Baptist Church would be arrested, so Blake cancelled the march and directed people into the church for the memorial service instead; in his testimony, mounted officers with bayonets and garbage trucks for hauling arrestees had massed in the surrounding community [src: 1, src: 5]. After the service, Blake recounts, the commissioner and two officers met him in the vestibule and beat him about the head, dragging him onto the lawn and leaving him for what they took to be dead [src: 1]. His family feared sending him to local white doctors and took him to Dallas for medical care [src: 1]. The Shreveport campaign sat at the western anchor of a broader Louisiana civil rights organizing arc that ran through New Orleans (where Rudy Lombard and the CORE chapter at Southern University at New Orleans had staged the September 1960 Canal Street sit-ins that produced the Supreme Court case Lombard v. Louisiana) and through Bogalusa in Washington Parish (where Fletcher Anderson, in the catalog as entry 25 with Cynthia Baker Anderson, was a founding member of the 1965 Deacons for Defense and Justice chapter that protected NAACP demonstrators against Klan violence at the Crown-Zellerbach paper mill) [src: 6, src: 7]. The Louisiana arc is the subject of Adam Fairclough's monograph Race and Democracy: The Civil Rights Struggle in Louisiana, 1915-1972 (University of Georgia Press, 1995), which centers the NAACP rather than the youth-led organizations of the mid-1960s and traces the persistence of local protest infrastructure across decades [src: 8].
legacy_and_reception: Blake's pastorate at Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport ran from 1966 until shortly before his death and shaped the church into a community-development institution beyond its pulpit role; he led the construction of Canaan Village affordable housing and the Harry Blake Life Center, both bearing his name in Shreveport [src: 5]. He served as president of the Louisiana Baptist State Convention and as general secretary of the National Baptist Convention USA, Inc., positions that made him one of the senior figures in the Louisiana Black Baptist denominational leadership through the 1990s and 2000s [src: 2]. Blake died April 8, 2020 in Shreveport at the age of eighty-five, his death attributed by family and local press accounts to COVID-19 during the first wave of the pandemic in Louisiana [src: 1, src: 5]. In 2022 the City of Shreveport issued a formal apology for the 1963 police beating at Little Union Baptist Church, more than fifty-eight years after the attack and two years after Blake's death; the apology was reported in conjunction with the seventieth-anniversary retrospective coverage by KTBS [src: 5]. His oral history with the Library of Congress, recorded by David P. Cline in Shreveport on October 3, 2013, is part of the Civil Rights History Project collection (AFC 2010/039) at the American Folklife Center [src: 1].

sources.title: Harry Blake oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Shreveport, Louisiana, 2013 October 03
